user_goals_summary: This user writes to their engineering team as a peer-leader, not a corporate mouthpiece. They prioritize framing messages around the reader's lived experience and concrete situation, sequencing information so acknowledgment earns the news rather than softening it retroactively, and ending on forward momentum rather than sentiment.

rubric.name: Reader-Centered Framing
rubric.description: The message is framed around what it means for the reader — their situation, their disruptions, their plans — not around the sender's values, commitments, or organizational rationale. When the 'why' names something concrete about the reader's experience, it lands as acknowledgment; when it centers the sender, it reads as self-explanation.

dimensions.label: Impact descriptions reference what the reader is actually experiencing or dealing with, not abstract outcomes
Rationale is not framed around the sender's or organization's values or commitments
Explicitly acknowledges what the message costs or asks of the reader
Language addresses the reader's situation rather than explaining the sender's position

rubric.name: Emotional Sequencing
rubric.description: The order in which information arrives shapes how the reader receives it. Acknowledgment and context come before the news or ask — they set the frame rather than chasing after it. Each section builds on the emotional state the previous section creates.

dimensions.label: Context or acknowledgment appears before the key news or ask, not after
The opening sets a frame that shapes how the reader receives what follows
Acknowledgment is not positioned as damage control after the headline has already landed
Each section flows naturally from the emotional state established by the prior one

rubric.name: Concrete Specificity
rubric.description: Impact, reasoning, and disruptions are named in tangible, specific terms — the actual texture of the situation — rather than in abstract quality or outcome language. 'Dependencies lined up, people informed' over 'undo the work that got us here.'

dimensions.label: Disruptions or stakes are described in terms of specific, real-world consequences (schedules, dependencies, downstream conversations)
Avoids abstract quality or outcome language ('getting this right,' 'undo the work') as the primary framing
References the actual texture of the reader's situation rather than generalized statements

rubric.name: Forward-Momentum Closings
rubric.description: Endings land on something that moves people forward — a next step, an invitation, a clear action — rather than trailing into sentiment, gratitude, or motivational language. The closing puts the ball in the reader's court.

dimensions.label: The closing moves the reader toward a next step or invitation rather than signing off with sentiment
No motivational-poster language, generic affirmations, or canned encouragement
The ending puts agency in the reader's hands (e.g., invites questions, opens a door)
The closing is clean and doesn't linger or add unnecessary padding

rubric.name: Human Voice
rubric.description: The message reads like it's from a person finishing a thought, not from a project tracker, corporate template, or status report. Prose is preferred over bureaucratic structure when it can carry the same information with more warmth and directness.

dimensions.label: Uses flowing prose where bullet lists would make the message feel like a status report
The overall message reads like a person talking to their team, not an institutional communication
Avoids corporate communication clichés and canned phrases
Sentences have natural, conversational rhythm rather than formal or bureaucratic cadence

rubric.name: Concise Consolidation
rubric.description: Multiple related items resolve into one clear, unified statement rather than fanning out into separate line items. Information is covered efficiently — every section serves a purpose and nothing is over-specified when a consolidated summary does the job.

dimensions.label: Related action items or details are consolidated into clear, unified statements rather than broken into separate components
Next steps and details are not over-specified when a brief summary would suffice
Every section serves a clear purpose with no padding or filler

coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is reader-centeredness: every framing choice, every sequencing decision, and every word choice is evaluated by whether it speaks to the reader's actual experience or defaults to the sender's perspective. They have a strong instinct for detecting abstraction masquerading as empathy — 'undo the work' sounds empathetic but is still organizational framing; 'dependencies lined up, people informed' is what actually lands. When in doubt, name the concrete over the aspirational, and end on momentum rather than warmth.

They prioritize framing messages around the reader's lived experience and concrete situation, sequencing information so acknowledgment earns the news rather than softening it retroactively, and ending on forward momentum rather than sentiment.
coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is reader-centeredness: every framing choice, every sequencing decision, and every word choice is evaluated by whether it speaks to the reader's actual experience or defaults to the sender's perspective. They have a strong instinct for detecting abstraction masquerading as empathy — 'undo the work' sounds empathetic but is still organizational framing; 'dependencies lined up, people informed' is what actually lands. When in doubt, name the concrete over the aspirational, and end on momentum rather than warmth.

rubric.name: Emotional Sequencing
rubric.description: Information arrives in a specific order: acknowledge the effort or situation first (one or two short sentences), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters, then give one concrete next step. Violating this order — even if all the pieces are present — breaks the flow. This isn't just about being considerate; it's about making the message actually land. Brevity should come from word choice and trimming, not from merging sentences with punctuation that changes the tone.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment or situation framing appears before the key news or ask, not after

Opening acknowledgment is brief — one or two short sentences — not an extended preamble. Do not merge sentences mechanically (e.g. with an em dash) to hit a sentence count; two short, natural sentences are preferable to one forced one.
Acknowledgment is not positioned as damage control after the headline has already landed
The 'why it matters' section appears before the next step, not after or mixed in
There is exactly one concrete next step at the end, not a list of follow-ups

rubric.name: Reader-Centered Framing
rubric.description: The 'why it matters' is always framed around what it costs or disrupts for the reader — their plans, their team, their timeline — not around the sender's values, commitments, or organizational rationale. 'This matters because your team planned around this date' not 'This matters because we want quality.' Drafts that center the sender's reasoning feel tone-deaf even when technically correct.

dimensions.label: Stakes are described in terms of what the reader is actually dealing with — schedules, dependencies, downstream conversations
Rationale does not center the sender's values, commitments, or organizational priorities
Explicitly acknowledges what the message costs or asks of the reader
Language addresses the reader's situation rather than explaining the sender's position

rubric.name: Action Ownership
rubric.description: When something needs to happen, there is one clear owner with a deadline — not a group ask. 'Everyone please review' is a non-action. At the same time, action items should not be over-specified in ways that assume org structure details the email shouldn't contain. The goal is clarity of ownership, not a task breakdown.

dimensions.label: There is one named or clearly implied owner for the next step, not a group or committee
The next step includes a concrete deadline or timeframe
Avoids 'everyone please...' or other diffuse asks that produce no clear accountability

Action items are not broken into sub-tasks or over-specified in ways that assume internal org details. Only flag over-specification when the speculative detail is unambiguously present and its removal doesn't change the meaning of the ask.

rubric.name: Forward-Momentum Closings
rubric.description: The closing lands on the next step or an open door — nothing after it. No 'thanks for your patience,' no motivational filler, no trailing sentiment. Any gratitude or warmth that comes after the CTA weakens the email. The last thing the reader sees should be something that moves things forward.

dimensions.label: The closing is the next step or an invitation — not a sentiment or sign-off phrase
No additional sentences appear after the call to action
No motivational-poster language, generic affirmations, or 'thanks for your patience' type phrases
The closing is clean — no padding, no lingering

rubric.name: Conciseness and Directness
rubric.description: Short paragraphs, no walls of text, no over-explanation. Every sentence earns its place. Related points are consolidated rather than fanned out into separate line items. The email should feel like it respects the reader's time.

dimensions.label: Paragraphs are short — typically 1-3 sentences
Related details are consolidated rather than broken into separate bullet points or line items
Every section serves a clear purpose — no padding or filler
Reasoning is stated once, cleanly — not repeated or belabored

rubric.name: Human Voice
rubric.description: The message reads like a person finishing a thought, not a corporate template or status report. No jargon, no passive-aggressive tone, no excessive exclamation marks. Professional but not stiff — the kind of email a sharp colleague would send.

dimensions.label: Free of corporate jargon and canned phrases
Reads like a person talking to their team, not an institutional communication
Tone is direct and neutral — no passive-aggressive undertones
Exclamation marks are used sparingly or not at all
Sentences have natural, conversational rhythm — not formal or bureaucratic cadence

Values treating readers as intelligent adults — no over-explanation, no sentiment padding, no vague ownership. Tone is direct and human, never corporate or institutional.

rubric.name: Emotional Sequencing
rubric.description: Information arrives in a specific order: acknowledge the effort or situation first (one or two short sentences), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters to the reader, then give one concrete next step. Violating this order — even if all the pieces are present — breaks the flow. Brevity should come from word choice and trimming, not from merging sentences with punctuation that changes the tone.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment or situation framing appears before the key news or ask, not after

Opening acknowledgment is brief — one or two short sentences — not an extended preamble. Do not merge sentences mechanically (e.g. with an em dash) to hit a sentence count; two short, natural sentences are preferable to one forced one
Acknowledgment is not positioned as damage control after the headline has already landed
The 'why it matters' section appears before the next step, not after or mixed in
There is exactly one concrete next step at the end, not a list of follow-ups

rubric.name: Reader-Centered Framing
rubric.description: The 'why it matters' is always framed around the reader's concrete reality — what it costs them (bad news) or what it unlocks for them (good news). Their time, their workflows, their team, their downstream conversations — not the sender's values, commitments, or organizational rationale. When this section names something specific and tangible about the reader's situation, it earns whatever ask or action follows.

dimensions.label: Impact is described in terms of the reader's concrete situation — their time, plans, workflows, dependencies, or downstream conversations
Value or disruption is named with concrete specifics — hours saved, people affected, workflows changed — not abstract benefits or quality statements
Rationale does not center the sender's values, commitments, or organizational priorities
Language addresses the reader's situation rather than explaining the sender's position

rubric.name: Action Ownership
rubric.description: When something needs to happen, there is one clear owner — a specific named person or the reader themselves — with a concrete deadline. 'Have someone on your IT team' is how things don't get done. Specify WHO and WHEN clearly; don't break the action into sub-tasks or assume process details the email shouldn't contain.

dimensions.label: There is one named or clearly identified person responsible for the next step — not a vague team, role, or 'someone on your team'
The next step includes a concrete deadline or timeframe
Avoids 'everyone please...' or other diffuse asks that produce no clear accountability
Action items are not broken into sub-tasks or process steps — specify who and when, not how

rubric.name: Forward-Momentum Closings
rubric.description: The closing lands on the next step or an open door — nothing after it. No 'thanks for your patience,' no motivational filler, no explanatory reasoning the reader can infer themselves. Once you've made the ask with a deadline, stop. Any sentence after the CTA — whether sentiment or justification — dilutes it.

dimensions.label: The closing is the next step or an invitation — not a sentiment or sign-off phrase
No additional sentences appear after the call to action — not sentiment, not reasoning, not justification
Does not add explanatory reasoning after the ask that the reader can infer from context (e.g., explaining why a deadline matters when the reason is obvious)
No motivational-poster language, generic affirmations, or 'thanks for your patience' type phrases

rubric.name: Conciseness and Directness
rubric.description: Short paragraphs, no walls of text, no over-explanation. Every sentence earns its place. Related points are consolidated rather than fanned out into separate line items. Trusts the reader to connect obvious dots — does not explain reasoning they can infer from context.

dimensions.label: Paragraphs are short — typically 1-3 sentences
Related details are consolidated rather than broken into separate bullet points or line items
Every section serves a clear purpose — no padding or filler
Reasoning is stated once, cleanly — not repeated or belabored
Does not explain reasoning or justification the reader can infer from context — treats the reader as an intelligent adult

rubric.name: Human Voice
rubric.description: The message reads like a person finishing a thought, not a corporate template or status report. No jargon, no passive-aggressive tone, no excessive exclamation marks. Professional but not stiff — the kind of email a sharp colleague would send.

dimensions.label: Free of corporate jargon and canned phrases

Tone is direct and neutral — no passive-aggressive undertones

Action ownership must point at a real, specific person; vague team references are treated as non-actions.

rubric.name: Human Voice
rubric.description: The message reads like a person finishing a thought, not a corporate template. No jargon, no passive-aggressive tone, no excessive exclamation marks. Professional but not stiff — the kind of email a sharp colleague would send.

dimensions.label: Free of corporate jargon and canned phrases
Reads like a person talking to a peer, not an institutional communication
Tone is direct and neutral — no passive-aggressive undertones
Exclamation marks are used sparingly or not at all
Sentences have natural, conversational rhythm — not formal or bureaucratic cadence

rubric.name: Conciseness and Directness
rubric.description: Short paragraphs, no walls of text, no over-explanation. Every sentence earns its place. Trusts the reader to connect obvious dots — does not spell out reasoning an intelligent adult can infer from context.

dimensions.label: Paragraphs are short — typically 1-3 sentences
Related details are consolidated rather than broken into unnecessary bullet points or line items
Every section serves a clear purpose — no padding or filler
Reasoning is stated once, cleanly — not repeated or belabored
Does not explain reasoning the reader can infer from context — treats the reader as an intelligent adult

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: structural beats (acknowledge → news → why it matters → ask) must stay as separate sentences — never collapse for brevity. This user's primary trade-off is always pacing over conciseness. They treat the 'why it matters' paragraph as the section that earns the right to make the ask — if it doesn't land, the whole email fails. When they push back, it's almost always about something feeling like it's talking AT the reader rather than TO them — whether that's corporate framing, vague delegation, or trailing justification that implies the reader can't figure things out.

rubric.name: Emotional Sequencing
rubric.description: Information arrives in a specific order: acknowledge the effort or situation first, then deliver the news, then explain why it matters to the reader, then give one concrete next step. Violating this order — even if all the pieces are present — breaks the flow. Structurally distinct beats (acknowledgment, news, impact, ask) must remain separate — never collapse them into a single sentence. Emotional pacing takes priority over brevity.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment or situation framing appears before the key news or ask, not after

Opening acknowledgment is one or two short, natural sentences — never merged with the news into one sentence using an em dash or other mechanical compression. Two clean sentences are always preferred over one forced merge.
Acknowledgment is not positioned as damage control after the headline has already landed — keeping acknowledgment and news as separate sentences is intentional pacing, not retroactive softening
The 'why it matters' section appears before the next step, not after or mixed in
There is exactly one concrete next step at the end, not a list of follow-ups

rubric.name: Reader-Centered Framing
rubric.description: The 'why it matters' is always framed around the reader's concrete reality — what it costs them or what it unlocks for them. Their time, their workflows, their team, their downstream conversations — not the sender's values, commitments, or organizational rationale. This section earns whatever ask follows. If the 'why' could have been written by someone explaining their own priorities rather than the reader's situation, it's wrong.

dimensions.label: Impact is described in terms of the reader's concrete situation — their time, plans, workflows, dependencies, or downstream conversations
Value or disruption is named with concrete specifics — not abstract benefits or quality statements
Rationale does not center the sender's values, commitments, or organizational priorities
The 'why it matters' section creates enough context that the subsequent ask or action feels justified and natural, not abrupt

rubric.name: Action Ownership
rubric.description: When something needs to happen, there is one clear owner — a specific named person or the reader themselves — with a concrete deadline. Group asks produce no accountability, and vague delegation ('have someone on your team') is how things don't get done. Keep accountability with the direct recipient; don't reach past them into their team's structure.

dimensions.label: There is one named or clearly identified person responsible for the next step — not a vague team, role, or 'someone on your team'
The next step includes a concrete deadline or timeframe — not a preference or suggestion
Avoids 'everyone please...' or other diffuse asks that produce no clear accountability
Does not name the recipient's direct reports or team members in the CTA — accountability stays with the recipient, who decides how to delegate internally
CTA tone matches the authority dynamic — imperative when the sender has authority, direct request when they don't — but never phrased as optional when it isn't

rubric.name: Conciseness and Directness
rubric.description: Short paragraphs, no walls of text, no over-explanation. Every sentence earns its place. Trusts the reader to connect obvious dots — does not spell out reasoning an intelligent adult can infer from context.

dimensions.label: Paragraphs are short — typically 1-3 sentences
Related details are consolidated rather than broken into unnecessary bullet points or line items
Every section serves a clear purpose — no padding or filler
Reasoning is stated once, cleanly — not repeated or belabored
Does not explain reasoning the reader can infer from context — treats the reader as an intelligent adult

rubric.name: Forward-Momentum Closings
rubric.description: The closing lands on the next step or an open door — nothing after it. No 'thanks for your patience,' no motivational filler, no trailing sentiment, no explanatory reasoning the reader can figure out themselves. Once the ask is made, stop. Any sentence after the CTA — whether sentiment or justification — dilutes it.

dimensions.label: The closing is the next step or an invitation — not a sentiment or sign-off phrase
No additional sentences appear after the call to action — not sentiment, not reasoning, not justification
Does not add explanatory reasoning after the ask that the reader can infer from context
No motivational-poster language, generic affirmations, or 'thanks for your patience' type phrases

rubric.description: The message reads like a person finishing a thought, not a corporate template. No jargon, no passive-aggressive tone, no excessive exclamation marks. Professional but not stiff — the kind of email a sharp colleague would send.

Reads like a person talking to a peer, not an institutional communication
Tone is direct and neutral — no passive-aggressive undertones
Exclamation marks are used sparingly or not at all

writing_type: professional emails (internal and external stakeholder communications)
user_goals_summary: Emails should follow a precise emotional sequence — acknowledge, deliver, explain why it matters to the reader, then make a clean ask. The 'why it matters' section must be framed around the reader's concrete situation, not the sender's priorities, and it should earn whatever ask follows. Closings end on the action and stop — no trailing sentiment, reasoning, or filler.
coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: structural beats (acknowledge → news → why it matters → ask) must stay as separate sentences — never collapse for brevity. This user's primary trade-off is always pacing over conciseness. They treat the 'why it matters' paragraph as the section that earns the right to make the ask — if it doesn't land, the whole email fails. When they push back, it's almost always about something feeling like it's talking AT the reader rather than TO them — whether that's corporate framing, vague delegation, or trailing justification that implies the reader can't figure things out.

user_goals_summary: Writes emails that acknowledge the reader's situation before delivering news, frame impact around the reader's concrete reality (not the sender's priorities), and close on a single clear action with a named owner and hard deadline. Values natural emotional pacing over mechanical brevity, and treats every sentence after the CTA as dilution.

rubric.name: Human Voice
rubric.description: The message reads like a person finishing a thought, not a corporate template. No jargon, no passive-aggressive tone, no excessive exclamation marks. Professional but not stiff — the kind of email a sharp colleague would send.

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: separate structural beats (acknowledgment → news → impact → ask), single named owner with hard deadline on every CTA, and absolutely nothing after the final ask. This user treats trailing sentiment or reasoning after the CTA as the single fastest way to weaken an email. When in doubt about acknowledgment length, favor warmth and natural pacing over forced compression — two short sentences always beat one awkward merged sentence. When the action involves someone on the recipient's team, keep the CTA pointed at the recipient and let them handle internal delegation.
